title: Chickweed compress for itching
subtitle: A cooling external application for mild itch
excerpt: A strong infusion of dried chickweed, cooled and applied as a compress to mildly itchy skin. One of the safest and most accessible cooling skin herbs in the western tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, chickweed entry: the cooling emollient tradition, the itch and irritation uses. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Stellaria media, the soothing herb and emollient external tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The infusion can be made ahead and stored in the fridge for 24 hours. Cool in the fridge for maximum cooling effect on the skin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that softens and soothes skin. Chickweed is one of the most gentle emollient herbs in western use, safe for sensitive, reactive, or eczema-prone skin.

content.text: Chickweed (Stellaria media) is a mild, cooling

herb, the gentlest of all the skin herbs in the western tradition and with in practice no contraindications for external use. Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer) records it as a traditional remedy for itching skin, eczema, and irritated patches. A strong infusion, cooled in the fridge, and applied as a cold compress to itchy skin gives noticeable relief within a few minutes. The cooling and gel-forming quality of the infusion is the action. It is appropriate for mild eczema, prickly heat, dry itchy patches, and mild hives on unbroken skin.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Chickweed has no known safety concerns for external use at standard doses. It is among the most benign herbs in the western tradition.

Skin conditions that need medical attention. Severe, widespread, or rapidly worsening eczema; skin rashes with fever; hives accompanied by throat tightening or difficulty breathing (anaphylaxis); or itching associated with jaundice, liver disease, or kidney disease all need medical assessment. Chickweed compress is for mild, localised skin itch only.

content.text: Place the dried chickweed in a saucepan. Pour over the hot water, cover, and steep for 15 minutes. Strain through a sieve. Transfer the strained infusion to the fridge and cool completely, 10-20 minutes. A cold compress is more effective than a warm one for itching, since cooling calms the itch reflex. Soak a clean cloth (cotton or linen) in the cool infusion, wring out lightly, and apply to the itching area. Re-soak and reapply as the cloth warms. Continue for 20-30 minutes.

Apply as needed, up to three or four times a day. There is no upper limit on applications for chickweed compress; it is safe for repeated use on unbroken skin. The compress session is 20-30 minutes each time. Suitable for adults and children of all ages for external use.
The prepared infusion keeps 24 hours in the fridge in a sealed container. Dried chickweed stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
Do not apply to infected skin or open sores. Any skin rash with fever, difficulty breathing, or throat tightening is a medical emergency.
